critic_raw:
This experimental design for SDOH-enhanced hospital readmission prediction has strengths but several important methodological gaps.

First, the model comparison is confounded. The experiment compares a gradient-boosted decision tree model (with SDOH + clinical features) against a logistic regression model (clinical features only). This conflates two factors: model family (gradient boosting vs. logistic regression) and feature set (SDOH + clinical vs. clinical only). If the gradient-boosted model outperforms logistic regression, it is impossible to determine whether the improvement is due to SDOH features or the more powerful model architecture. The appropriate comparison requires at minimum four conditions: gradient boosting with clinical only, gradient boosting with SDOH + clinical, logistic regression with clinical only, logistic regression with SDOH + clinical.

Second, stratified random split on patient data spanning five years. Hospital readmission prediction models are deployed prospectively — a model trained on 2019-2022 patients predicts for 2023 patients. If training includes patients admitted in 2023 and test includes patients admitted in 2019, the model may learn from future hospital policies, treatment protocols, and SDOH availability patterns. A temporal split is more appropriate.

Third, SDOH features such as income, education, and housing status are notoriously subject to missing data — these fields may be missing non-randomly (sicker or less socioeconomically advantaged patients may have incomplete records). The design mentions imputation but does not specify the imputation strategy or whether missingness patterns are analyzed.

Fourth, AUC-ROC as the primary metric is reasonable but may obscure clinically relevant distinctions. At the operating point used for clinical decision-making (flagging high-risk patients for intervention), precision-recall tradeoffs are more relevant than overall discrimination. AUC-PR or a fixed-recall precision metric would better align with clinical deployment.

Fifth, EHR data spanning five years may include ICD code transitions (ICD-9 to ICD-10 in the US occurred in 2015; earlier shifts may affect international data). Mixing codes without standardization could introduce systematic noise.
all_issues_raised: Confounded comparison: model family and feature set changed simultaneously
Temporal leakage from random split on multi-year hospital data
SDOH missingness likely non-random; imputation strategy not specified
AUC-ROC primary metric misaligned with clinical decision-making operating points
ICD code version transitions over 5-year span not addressed

round_transcripts.critic: This design has five issues: confounded model-vs-feature comparison, temporal leakage from random split, non-random SDOH missingness, AUC-ROC misalignment with clinical operating points, and ICD code transition handling.
round_transcripts.defender:
The critique identifies real methodological issues. Let me evaluate each.

On confounded comparison: this is the most critical issue and the critic is fully correct. Comparing gradient boosting + SDOH against logistic regression + clinical features cannot isolate the value of SDOH. The design must include at minimum a gradient boosting model trained without SDOH to isolate the feature contribution. I concede fully.

On temporal split: for a model predicting readmissions prospectively, a temporal split is more rigorous. However, patient readmission patterns are partly driven by chronic condition states that are relatively stable over time, so the temporal leakage effect may be smaller than in other time-series settings. I concede a temporal split should be the primary approach with random split as a secondary robustness check.

On SDOH missingness: fully conceded. SDOH data missingness is frequently non-random in clinical datasets, and a naive imputation without analyzing the missingness mechanism can introduce systematic bias. The design should include missing data mechanism analysis (MCAR, MAR, MNAR).

On AUC-ROC vs. operating-point metrics: partially conceded. AUC-ROC is a standard and defensible metric for readmission prediction, but the critic is correct that clinical deployment focuses on a specific operating point. AUPRC or precision at a fixed recall threshold should be included as secondary metrics at minimum.

On ICD code transitions: this is a valid practical concern for US data in the 2015+ period. The design should specify how ICD code versioning is handled — either by excluding pre-transition data, standardizing to one version, or using concept mapping.

round_transcripts.critic:
The defender has conceded all five issues. The most critical ones — confounded comparison and temporal split — are now clearly flagged as required design amendments. The SDOH missingness mechanism analysis is also correctly identified as needed. ICD code handling is a valid practical concern.

The residual question is whether, after these corrections, the hypothesis (SDOH improves readmission prediction) is testable. Yes, it is — but the design needs four model conditions, temporal split, and SDOH missingness analysis before results can be interpreted.
round_transcripts.defender: Agreed on all points. The design requires four model conditions to isolate SDOH contribution, a temporal split, and a missingness mechanism analysis. With these corrections, the hypothesis is directly and reliably testable. The SDOH improvement signal, if present, will be attributable to the features rather than the model family.

round_transcripts.critic: The debate has converged. All major issues are acknowledged. The design requires substantial amendment before valid conclusions can be drawn about SDOH feature value.
round_transcripts.defender: Agreed. The required amendments are: four-condition comparison, temporal split as primary, SDOH missingness analysis, and ICD code versioning handling.

all_issues_adjudicated: Confounded comparison: gradient boosting + SDOH vs. logistic regression + clinical confounds model family and features
Temporal leakage from random split on multi-year hospital data
SDOH missingness non-random: missingness mechanism analysis required
